sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 150, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 149 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 5.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
UP x2 didn't move the right green! The right green is still at (36-38, 21-23). This means UP doesn't move the right green, even though it's the leftmost.
Wait, maybe the selection rule is different. Let me re-examine.
Looking at the early transitions:
- Initial: left green at (30-32, 18-20), right green at (30-32, 27-29). LEFT moves the left green (18-20 < 27-29).
- After LEFT x3: left green at (30-32, 9-11), right green at (30-32, 27-29). LEFT moves the left green.
- After DOWN: left green at (30-32, 27-29), right green at (33-35, 18-20). Now the right green is at col 18-20 < 27-29, so it's the leftmost. But does LEFT move the right green?
Let me check transition 7:
Before: left green at (30-32, 27-29), right green at (33-35, 18-20)
Action: LEFT
After: left green at (30-32, 24-26), right green at (33-35, 18-20)
So LEFT moved the left green (at 27-29), not the right green (at 18-20)! This contradicts my assumption that the leftmost green is the one being moved.
Wait, let me re-examine. After DOWN (transition 6), the left green moved to (30-32, 27-29) and the right green moved to (33-35, 18-20). The left green is at col 27-29 and the right green is at col 18-20. The right green is the leftmost, but LEFT moved the left green!
So the selection rule is NOT based on leftmost. Let me re-examine the selection rule.
Looking at the initial state: left green at (30-32, 18-20), right green at (30-32, 27-29). LEFT moves the left green.
After DOWN: left green at (30-32, 27-29), right green at (33-35, 18-20). LEFT moves the left green (at 27-29).
So the selection rule is: the left green (the one that was originally on the left) is always the one being moved by LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN, regardless of its current position!
Wait, but that doesn't make sense either. Let me look at more transitions.
Transition 18: UP caused a SWAP. Before: left green at (30-32, 27-29), right green at (33-35, 12-14). After: left green at (30-32, 12-14), right green at (30-32, 27-29).
So UP swapped the greens. The left green moved from (30-32, 27-29) to (30-32, 12-14), and the right green moved from (33-35, 12-14) to (30-32, 27-29).
Hmm, but the segmentation doesn't track which green is "left" and which is "right" across frames. The greens are identified by their position and color, not by a persistent ID.
Let me re-examine the selection rule by looking at the current state:
- Green 1: (30-32, 24-26), 8 pixels
- Green 2: (36-38, 21-23), 8 pixels
UP didn't move either green. This is strange.
Wait, maybe UP only moves the topmost green when both are at the same column. In the current state, the greens are at different columns (24-26 vs 21-23). So UP doesn't move either green because they're not aligned.
Let me re-examine the early transitions:
- Initial: left green at (30-32, 18-20), right green at (30-32, 27-29). Both at row 30-32. LEFT moves the left green (18-20).
- After LEFT x3: left green at (30-32, 9-11), right green at (30-32, 27-29). Both at row 30-32. LEFT moves the left green (9-11).
- After DOWN: left green at (30-32, 27-29), right green at (33-35, 18-20). Different rows. LEFT moves the left green (27-29).
So the selection rule seems to be: the left green (the one that was originally on the left, or the one with the smaller column) is the one being moved by LEFT/RIGHT. UP/DOWN might have different rules.
But in the current state, UP didn't move either green. Let me check if UP has a specific condition.
Looking at transition 18: UP caused a SWAP when the left green was at (30-32, 27-29) and the right green was at (33-35, 12-14). The rows were 30-32 and 33-35, which are adjacent (32 and 33 are consecutive).
In the current state, the greens are at rows 30-32 and 36-38. The rows are not adjacent (32 and 36 are not consecutive). So UP doesn't cause a swap because the greens are too far apart.
So the selection rule for UP/DOWN might be:
- UP/DOWN only works when the greens are at adjacent rows (e.g., 30-32 and 33-35).
- When greens are at adjacent rows, UP/DOWN causes a swap.
But wait, in the initial state, both greens were at row 30-32. DOWN caused the left green to stay at row 30-32 and the right green to move to row 33-35. So DOWN when both greens are at the same row causes a specific swap pattern.
Let me re-examine the DOWN mechanic:
- Transition 6: Before: left green at (30-32, 18-20), right green at (30-32, 27-29). Both at row 30-32.
- After DOWN: left green at (30-32, 27-29), right green at (33-35, 18-20).
- So DOWN swaps the columns and moves the right green DOWN 3 rows.
And the UP mechanic:
- Transition 18: Before: left green at (30-32, 27-29), right green at (33-35, 12-14).
- After UP: left green at (30-32, 12-14), right green at (30-32, 27-29).
- So UP swaps the columns and moves the right green UP 3 rows.
So the swap mechanic is:
- DOWN when both greens at same row: left green takes right green's column, right green moves DOWN 3 rows to left green's column.
- UP when greens at adjacent rows (30-32 and 33-35): left green takes right green's column, right green moves UP 3 rows to left green's row.
Wait, but transition 18 had the left green at (30-32, 27-29) and the right green at (33-35, 12-14). After UP, the left green is at (30-32, 12-14) and the right green is at (30-32, 27-29). So the left green moved from col 27-29 to col 12-14, and the right green moved from row 33-35 to row 30-32 and from col 12-14 to col 27-29.
This is confusing. Let me just focus on the current state and try to figure out what actions work.
Current state:
- Green A: (30-32, 24-26), 8 pixels
- Green B: (36-38, 21-23), 8 pixels
UP didn't work. Let me try RIGHT to see if it moves Green A (the one at 24-26).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# UP didn't move anything. Greens are at different rows (30-32 and 36-38), not adjacent.
# Let me check what RIGHT does - it should move the leftmost green
# Current: Green A at (30-32, 24-26), Green B at (36-38, 21-23)
# Green B is leftmost (col 21-23 < 24-26)

result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("After RIGHT:")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        r_min, c_min = n['boundary'][0]
        r_max, c_max = n['boundary'][2]
        center_r = (r_min + r_max) // 2
        center_c = (c_min + c_max) // 2
        dot_char = ascii_lines[center_r][center_c]
        print(f"  Green at ({r_min}-{r_max}, {c_min}-{c_max}), center=({center_r},{center_c})={dot_char}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"No impact: {last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After RIGHT:
  Green at (30-32, 27-29), center=(31,28)=W, pixels=8
  Green at (36-38, 21-23), center=(37,22)=c, pixels=8
Board changed: True
No impact: False
